name: American Grotesk Compressed
classification_notes: A compressed grotesque derived from the Franklin Gothic lineage, sharing the same American vernacular grotesque DNA as American Grotesk but in a tightly compressed width suited to headlines and posters.
summary: The compressed cut of Kris Sowersby's Franklin Gothic reprisal, built across seven weights with matching italics for tight measures and large-scale display work.
description: Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black, each paired with an italic, in a tightly compressed width drawn from the Franklin Gothic tradition. The family shares its DNA with American Grotesk and American Grotesk Condensed, giving designers a coherent system across three widths. Designed for headlines, posters and editorial contexts where the warmth of American grotesque vernacular is needed in the narrowest possible footprint.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe American Grotesk Compressed as part of their broader American Grotesk family, a reprisal of Franklin Gothic framed against American graphic vernacular history and persuasive communication. Released alongside a wider reflection on truth, authorship and authenticity in the age of generative AI, the compressed cut offers 14 styles for demanding display and editorial applications.

name: American Grotesk
classification_notes: A grotesque sans in the tradition of Franklin Gothic, drawing on industrial American type foundry heritage with double-storey letterforms and a strong display character.
summary: A reprisal of Franklin Gothic by Kris Sowersby, rooted in American industrial type history and built for persuasive, editorial work.
description: Fourteen styles span seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving the family considerable range across editorial and campaign contexts. The design revisits Franklin Gothic's double-storey letterforms and vernacular grotesque character, updated for contemporary use without sanding away its historical grit. Released at a moment Klim frame as one of contested authorship and authenticity, the typeface extends into Condensed and Compressed cuts for tighter measures and poster-scale work.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe American Grotesk as a reprisal of Franklin Gothic, a typeface they position as central to American graphic vernacular history and persuasive communication. They situate the release against a present-day culture of personal truths and the growth of generative AI, framing it as a response to a moment when authority, authorship and authenticity are being redefined through an uncanny new form of Americana.

name: American Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed cut of a Franklin Gothic revival, sitting squarely in the American grotesque tradition with double-storey letterforms and industrial character suited to headlines and display use.
summary: The condensed width of Kris Sowersby's Franklin Gothic revival, offering fourteen styles from Thin to Black with matching italics for tight measures and headline work.
description: American Grotesk Condensed is the narrower companion to Sowersby's American Grotesk, sharing its roots in the ATF grotesque tradition and Morris Fuller Benton's original Franklin Gothic. Fourteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, making it well suited to editorial layouts, posters and campaign work where column width demands a tighter fit. The family sits alongside the standard and Compressed widths, giving designers a full range across the grotesque spectrum.
foundry_blurb: Klim frame American Grotesk Condensed as part of a reprisal of Franklin Gothic, a typeface they describe as central to American graphic vernacular history and persuasive communication. Released in a moment when authorship and authenticity are being redefined by generative AI and a culture of personal truths, the family draws on the ATF grotesque tradition while addressing the uncanny contours of contemporary Americana.

name: Domaine Display
classification_notes: A sharp, elegant serif blending French and British traditions, characterised by bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals, placing it firmly in the contemporary high-contrast serif category.
summary: A sharp, high-contrast serif drawing on French and British traditions, built around triangular bracketed serifs and hooked terminals for display use.
description: Twelve styles span six weights from Regular to Black, each with a matching italic informed by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série. The design balances figurative detailing — triangular serifs, hooked terminals — against calm horizontal head serifs that stabilise the letterforms at large sizes. Part of a wider Domaine family that includes a Text cut for smaller settings.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine Display as a sharp, elegant serif that blends traditional French and British genres into a contemporary aesthetic. Its curvaceous Latin detailing centres on gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals, while horizontal head serifs provide a stable ground for the figurative details. The italics are inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, conceived as a subtle and assured complement to the romans.

name: Die Grotesk
classification_notes: Die Grotesk is a modernist grotesque sans-serif shaped by the tradition of Helvetica, rooted in the study of original metal cuts and the rigour of modernist masters, placing it squarely in the neo-grotesque lineage.
summary: A variable grotesque by Klim that works through the long shadow of Helvetica, offering optical compensation across sizes via two variable axes.
description: Klim's first retail variable font, Die Grotesk sits in the neo-grotesque tradition with weight and optical axes that enable precise control across display and text settings. Sixteen static styles run from Hairline to Heavy with matching italics, each tuned for typographic texture at its intended size. The design holds Helvetica at arm's length, acknowledging its dominance without simply reproducing it.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Die Grotesk as shaped in the long shadow of Helvetica, a typeface both revered and resented, simultaneously banal and sublime. Rooted in the study of original metal cuts and the rigour of modernist masters, Die Grotesk is their first retail variable font, designed for perfect typographic texture across all sizes. They frame it as at once homage and critique, acknowledging the tension between creative integrity and commercial reality in a market dominated by commercial pressure and corporate monopoly.

name: Calibre
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a neo-grotesque sans serif, drawing inspiration from West Berlin street sign lettering and taking rationality from Nebiolo's Recta, placing it firmly in the neo-grotesque subgenre with geometric underpinnings softened by optical correction.
summary: A neo-grotesque drawn from West Berlin street sign lettering, applying optical correction to temper the engineer's geometry into something crisper and more typographically considered.
description: Calibre takes its cue from the elegant, clear lettering found on West Berlin street signs, a relatively unusual source for signage-inspired type. Rather than reproducing raw geometric forms, it borrows rationality from Nebiolo's Recta and applies typographic optical correction, arriving at a contemporary, crisp result. Designed alongside Metric, the two families share a fundamental geometry but diverge at key letterforms, giving Calibre its distinct neo-grotesque character across 14 styles from Thin to Black with matching italics.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Calibre as a neo-grotesque sans serif inspired by lettering on West Berlin street signs, letterforms they note are unusually elegant and clear for engineered signage. The design tempers geometric tendencies with typographic optical correction, drawing rationality from Nebiolo's Recta for a contemporary, crisp finish. It was designed alongside Metric, sharing a fundamental geometry but differing in the treatment of key letterforms.

name: Domaine Display Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed display serif drawing on traditional French and British serif conventions, with triangular bracketed serifs and hooked terminals that place it firmly in the high-contrast display category.
summary: A condensed display serif that melds French and British typographic traditions through triangular bracketed serifs and distinctive hooked terminals.
description: Domaine Display Condensed offers six weights from Regular to Black, each with a companion italic, across a condensed proportion suited to headlines and editorial use. The design's curvaceous Latin detailing, gently bracketed triangular serifs and hooked terminals give it a sharp elegance with historical grounding. Italics draw from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, lending a measured, assured quality to the sloped cuts.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine as a sharp, elegant serif that blends traditional French and British genres into a contemporary aesthetic. Its curvaceous Latin detailing centres on gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals, with horizontal head serifs providing a calm, stable ground. The italics are inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, offering a subtle and assured complement to the romans.

name: Domaine Display Narrow
classification_notes: A sharp, high-contrast serif with triangular bracketed serifs and hooked terminals drawn from French and British serif traditions, optimised for display use in a narrow width variant.
summary: A narrow display serif drawing on French and British serif traditions, with triangular bracketed serifs and hooked terminals that lend it a sharp, figurative character.
description: Domaine Display Narrow combines influences from traditional French and British serif genres into a contemporary display typeface, distinguished by gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals. Horizontal head serifs provide a stable counterbalance to the more figurative details, while the italics draw from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série for a quietly confident companion to the romans. Twelve styles run from Regular to Black with matching italics, suited to editorial, identity and branding work where a narrow measure is required.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine as a sharp, elegant serif that blends traditional French and British genres into a contemporary aesthetic. Its curvaceous Latin detailing centres on gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals, with horizontal head serifs providing a calm, stable ground. The italics are inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, designed as a subtle and assured complement to the romans.

name: Domaine Text
classification_notes: Sharp, elegantly bracketed triangular serifs with hooked terminals and horizontal head serifs point to a transitional serif tradition, blending French and British genre conventions into a contemporary form.
summary: A sharp, elegant serif blending French and British traditions, with triangular bracketed serifs, hooked terminals and italics drawn from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série.
description: Built around gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals, the design draws on both French and British serif traditions to produce a contemporary result that sits comfortably in long-form text. Horizontal head serifs steady the rhythm across the page, allowing the more characterful detailing to register without disrupting reading. The italics take their cue from Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, giving them a historical grounding that complements rather than mimics the romans. Available in five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine as a sharp, elegant serif that blends traditional French and British genres into a contemporary aesthetic. Its Latin detailing centres on gently bracketed triangular serifs and distinctive hooked terminals, with horizontal head serifs providing a calm, stable ground. The italics are inspired by Deberny & Peignot's Labeurs Ordinaires Série, described as a subtle and assured complement to the romans.

name: Domaine Sans Text
classification_notes: Domaine Sans belongs to the rare genre of sans serifs with pronounced stroke contrast, drawing on the optical refinements of its serif counterpart Domaine; the Text variant is optimised for smaller sizes.
summary: A high-contrast sans serif cut for text sizes, extending the Domaine family into an underexplored genre.
description: Domaine Sans Text explores the niche territory of sans serifs with visible stroke contrast, a companion to the serif Domaine family. Ten styles span five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, sized and spaced for sustained reading. The contrast gives it a precision and typographic tension rarely found in text sans serifs.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine Sans as venturing into another unpopular genre: sans serifs with contrast, positioning it as a companion exploration to Domaine's own foray into an underappreciated Latin typeface territory.

name: Domaine Sans Fine
classification_notes: Domaine Sans Fine belongs to the rare genre of sans serifs with significant stroke contrast, sitting between a classical serif and a geometric sans. Klim explicitly frame it as an exploration of this unpopular genre.
summary: A high-contrast sans-serif with eight weights and matching italics, designed as a deliberate foray into one of type design's least fashionable genres.
description: Domaine Sans Fine pursues the rare territory of sans serifs with visible stroke contrast, a genre Klim acknowledge as unpopular. Sixteen styles span Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, giving designers a broad tonal range from delicate to assertive. In use it appears across editorial, branding and cultural identity projects where a refined, contrast-driven sans is preferred over neutral grotesques.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine Sans Fine as an exploration into the unpopular genre of sans serifs with contrast, positioning it as a companion to Domaine, which itself explored another unfashionable territory in Latin typeface design.

name: Domaine Sans Display
classification_notes: Domaine Sans Display belongs to the rare genre of high-contrast sans serifs, combining the stroke modulation typical of classical serifs with a sans terminus. This places it in a distinct niche between humanist serif and sans-serif traditions.
summary: A high-contrast sans serif in the Domaine family, designed for display use with strong stroke modulation across eight weights.
description: Part of Klim's broader Domaine type system, the Display cut applies noticeable stroke contrast to a sans-serif structure, a combination Klim describe as an unpopular genre. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, making it well suited to editorial and fashion contexts where presence and refinement are both required. Pairs naturally with Domaine Display and Domaine Sans Text within the same system.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Domaine Sans Display as an exploration into an unpopular genre: sans serifs with contrast. It extends the Domaine family into sans-serif territory with the same emphasis on stroke modulation that defines the broader system, offering 16 styles from Thin to Black Italic.

name: Family
classification_notes: Family is a revival and extension of Clearface, an American serif with oldstyle influences, open forms and elliptical terminals. Its warm curves, sloped crossbar on the e and idiosyncratic details place it within the transitional serif tradition, updated for contemporary text use.
summary: A revival of Clearface by Morris Fuller and Linn Boyd Benton, retaining the original's easy-going curves and idiosyncrasies while adapting it for contemporary text setting.
description: Sowersby preserves what makes Clearface distinctive — the sloped e crossbar, elliptical terminals, and the abbreviated f and j overhangs — while harmonising inconsistencies in the heavier weights and making considered adjustments throughout. The italic follows Benton's simplified approach, keeping forms open and unfussy, with only small departures such as a descending f added to match the roman. Twelve styles span Light to Black with matching italics, and the family is available as a variable font across a continuous weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Family as an everyday typeface based on Clearface, originally designed by Morris Fuller and Linn Boyd Benton, father and son. The revival keeps Clearface's easy-going curves and characteristic idiosyncrasies while updating the design for modern use.

name: Epicene Display
classification_notes: Klim describe Epicene as Baroque, inspired by 18th-century punchcutters J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann; its exaggerated details and high contrast place it firmly in the Baroque/Transitional serif tradition optimised for display use.
summary: A Baroque display serif drawn from the work of Rosart and Fleischmann, with exaggerated details that reward both small text and large headline settings.
description: Epicene Display draws on the punchcutting traditions of J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, two 18th-century masters whose work shaped the Baroque style across continental Europe. Ten styles span five weights from Light to Black, each with matching italics, and the family pairs with Epicene Text for mixed-size typographic systems. Klim designed it with a deliberate cultural stance: that typefaces carry no inherent gender, giving Epicene its name and framing.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Epicene Display as part of a paired Baroque type system alongside Epicene Text, both inspired by 18th-century type designers J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann. They note that its exaggerated details add rigour at small sizes and vigour at large sizes, and frame the family around the cultural proposition that typefaces have no gender.

name: Epicene Text
classification_notes: Explicitly described as Baroque, inspired by 18th-century punchcutters J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, placing it firmly in the historical Baroque serif tradition with high contrast and exaggerated detailing.
summary: A Baroque text serif in five weights with italics, drawn from the 18th-century traditions of Rosart and Fleischmann and conceived deliberately outside typographic gender conventions.
description: Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, calibrated for use at text sizes where its exaggerated Baroque details add optical rigour rather than decorative noise. The design draws directly on the punchcutting work of J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann, both masters of the 18th-century Dutch and Flemish tradition. Klim released it alongside a companion Display family, the two together forming a single conceptual statement that typefaces carry no inherent gender.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Epicene Text and Display as Baroque typefaces inspired by 18th-century maestros J-F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischmann. Typographically, they note that Epicene's exaggerated details add rigour at small sizes and vigour at large sizes, while culturally the family makes one point: typefaces have no gender.

name: Financier Display
classification_notes: Financier Display draws from Eric Gill's Perpetua, a neoclassical serif with high contrast and stately proportions, placing it firmly in the transitional-to-classical tradition with a British editorial character.
summary: Drawn for the Financial Times redesign in 2014, this display serif takes its stately proportions from Eric Gill's Perpetua with italics that reconcile calligraphic tradition and mechanically sloped Romans.
description: Commissioned for the Financial Times in 2014, Financier Display is the headline counterpart to Financier Text, with both families rooted in Eric Gill's letterforms. The display cut takes Perpetua's high contrast and formal presence as its starting point, while the italics sit at a narrow, slight incline that blends calligraphic warmth with mechanical discipline. Twelve styles run from Light to Black with matching italics, and the family is available as a variable font across the weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Financier as an elegant, authoritative serif with an undeniable British heritage, drawn for the Financial Times redesign in 2014. Financier Display takes its aesthetic from Eric Gill's Perpetua, lending it a stately charm, while its italics reconcile mechanical sloped Romans with traditional calligraphic forms. The family is designed to work across all media, from wide-printed broadsheets to narrow mobile screens.

name: Feijoa
classification_notes: Feijoa is a humanist serif built on the principle that straight lines deaden letterforms, resulting in warmly curved, organic strokes that distinguish it from sharper transitional or neoclassical serifs. Its text-first design with a separate Display cut reinforces its humanist editorial orientation.
summary: The debut typeface from Klim Type Foundry, a humanist serif built around curvaceous letterforms that soften digital type's tendency toward mechanical sharpness.
description: Drawn on the principle that a straight line is a dead line, Feijoa uses warmly curved strokes to counter the harsh angles that can afflict screen and print type. The family runs across three text weights — Medium, Medium Italic and Bold — with a separate Display cut optimised for sizes above 16pt. All four styles include fanciful ligatures in the tradition of Zuzana Licko's Mrs Eaves.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Feijoa as their debut release, grounded in the belief that straight lines deaden letterforms. The curvaceous design avoids sharp angles detrimental to digital reproduction and aims to humanise the overall visual impression. Core text weights suit extended reading and book typography, while the Display cut targets headline use. All styles carry decorative ligatures inspired by Mrs Eaves.

name: Financier Text
classification_notes: Draws from Eric Gill's Solus and Joanna for its text cut, combining flat unbracketed serifs and beaked terminals with deep sharp joins; sits between old style and transitional conventions with a distinctly British pragmatism.
summary: A text serif drawn for the 2014 Financial Times redesign, rooted in Eric Gill's Solus and Joanna with flat unbracketed serifs and deep sharp joins suited to newsprint and screen alike.
description: Designed by Kris Sowersby for the Financial Times redesign launched in 2014, the text cut takes its aesthetic cues from Gill's Solus and Joanna rather than conventional newspaper serifs, resulting in more elegant proportions with a lower x-height and taller ascenders than the genre norm. Flat, unbracketed serifs and beaked terminals act as practical countermeasures for newsprint without relying on ink traps, while the italics reconcile sloped Roman forms with calligraphic tradition through occasional deliberate flourishes. Its four weights from Regular to Black support a variable font axis, making it equally viable across broadsheet print and narrow mobile screens.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Financier as an elegant, authoritative serif with undeniable British heritage, drawn for the FT redesign of 2014. The Text styles take pragmatic detailing from Gill's Solus and Joanna, using flat serifs and beaked terminals to survive the brutish conditions of newsprint, while the italics reconcile sloped Romans with calligraphic tradition. Klim note that Financier Text deliberately departs from typical newspaper proportions, opening up the ascenders and lowering the x-height to give the typeface a more elegant character suited to long-form reading across print and digital.

name: Founders Grotesk X-Condensed
classification_notes: An extra-condensed cut of a contemporary grotesque family drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series and Caslon's Doric, with Helvetica Halbfett spacing influence. Sits firmly in the grotesque tradition.
summary: The extra-condensed cut of Founders Grotesk, a family synthesised from Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series, Caslon's Doric and Helvetica's Halbfett spacing.
description: Five weights from Light to Bold draw on the narrow geometry and serpentine curves of early 20th century grotesques, refined with details from the Caslon Doric series and the tight spacing of Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts. The X-Condensed width pushes that compression further than the Condensed sibling, making it a natural choice for tight measures and large-scale headline work. Seen in editorial, cultural identity and poster contexts where strong vertical economy is required.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing principally on Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series for its rudimentary geometry, serpentine curves and narrow apertures, with further details from H. W. Caslon's Doric series and spacing strategies taken from Helvetica's Halbfett medium headline metal cuts of the late 1950s. The family is not a strict revival but a resolution of the best details from the last century into a large family designed for modern typography.

name: Founders Grotesk
classification_notes: Founders Grotesk draws directly from early 20th century grotesque sources including Millar & Richard and H. W. Caslon's Doric series, placing it squarely in the grotesque sans-serif tradition rather than neo-grotesque or humanist sub-families.
summary: A contemporary grotesque drawn from early 20th century sources including Millar & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric, and Helvetica's headline metal cuts.
description: Rather than reviving a single historical model, the family synthesises details from several early grotesque sources: the rudimentary geometry and serpentine curves of Millar & Richard, the detailing of Caslon's Doric series, and the tight spacing of Helvetica's Halbfett headline cuts from the late 1950s. The result is a ten-style family spanning Light to Bold with matching italics, designed to work as a practical text and display face for contemporary typography. It occupies the space between strict revival and original design, resolving a century of grotesque refinement into a single coherent system.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series for its geometry, serpentine curves and narrow apertures, Caslon's Doric series for further detailing, and the tight spacing strategies of Helvetica's Halbfett headline metal cuts from the late 1950s. They are clear that it is not intended as a strict revival, but as a resolution of the best details from the last century into a large family built for modern typography.

name: Founders Grotesk Condensed
classification_notes: A condensed cut of a contemporary grotesque family derived from early 20th century sources including Millar & Richard's Grotesque series and H. W. Caslon's Doric series, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif tradition.
summary: A condensed grotesque drawing from Millar & Richard's early 20th century series and H. W. Caslon's Doric, refined with Helvetica Halbfett spacing principles for modern use.
description: Five weights from Light to Bold give this condensed cut a versatile range for editorial and identity work where narrow measures are required. The design synthesises early grotesque sources with tight metal-cut spacing strategies, resulting in something that feels historical in its DNA but entirely purposeful in application. It sits within the broader Founders Grotesk family alongside Text, X-Condensed and standard width cuts.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Founders Grotesk as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th century Grotesque series for rudimentary geometry and serpentine curves, H. W. Caslon's Doric series for further detailing, and Helvetica's Halbfett headline metal cuts from the late 1950s for tight spacing strategies. The family is not intended as a strict revival but resolves the best details from the last century into a large family designed for modern typography.

name: Geograph
classification_notes: Klim describe Geograph as a contemporary geometric sans serif drawing on the idealism of Futura and the pragmatism of Super Grotesk, placing it firmly within the geometric sans tradition with grotesk pragmatism tempered in.
summary: A geometric sans commissioned for National Geographic, balancing Futura's idealism against the pragmatism of Super Grotesk across twelve styles with variable font support.
description: Designed originally for National Geographic, Geograph draws from the historic wellsprings of geometric and grotesk typefaces, referencing both the idealism of Futura and the practicality of Super Grotesk. Twelve styles span six weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and the family is available as a variable font. A suite of alternate letterforms allows typographers to shift texture and tone depending on context.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Geograph as a contemporary geometric sans serif originally designed for National Geographic, developed by revisiting the historic origins of geometric and grotesk typefaces. The design draws upon Futura's idealism and Super Grotesk's pragmatism, and includes a suite of alternate letterforms to allow flexible typographic textures and tones.

name: Founders Grotesk Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of Founders Grotesk, itself a grotesque sans rooted in Millar & Richard's early 20th-century Grotesque series. The mono classification reflects the fixed-pitch spacing applied to the grotesque letterforms.
summary: A monospaced companion to Founders Grotesk, distilling early 20th-century grotesque references into a fixed-pitch family across five weights.
description: Drawn from the same source material as Founders Grotesk, which synthesises Millar & Richard's Grotesque series, H. W. Caslon's Doric, and the tight spacing of Helvetica Halbfett metal cuts. The mono variant applies fixed-pitch spacing to those refined grotesque proportions, making it suited to code, tabular data and editorial contexts where monospacing is required. Five weights from Light to Bold give reasonable typographic range without italics.
foundry_blurb: Founders Grotesk Mono is a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th-century Grotesque series for rudimentary geometry and serpentine curves, H. W. Caslon's Doric series for additional detail, and Helvetica's Halbfett headline metal cuts from the late 1950s for tight spacing strategies. It is not intended as a strict revival, but resolves the best details from the last century into a family designed for modern typography.

name: Founders Grotesk Text
classification_notes: Founders Grotesk Text is a grotesque sans-serif drawing on early 20th-century British grotesque sources, including Millar & Richard and H. W. Caslon's Doric series, with spacing informed by Helvetica's metal cuts.
summary: A text-optimised cut of Founders Grotesk, blending early 20th-century British grotesque sources with spacing drawn from Helvetica's metal headline cuts.
description: Ten styles across five weights from Light to Bold, each with a matching italic, tuned for sustained reading rather than display use. The design synthesises geometry and aperture from Millar & Richard's Grotesque series, detailing from H. W. Caslon's Doric series, and tight spacing strategies borrowed from Helvetica Halbfett metal cuts of the late 1950s. The result sits between historical revival and contemporary practicality, making it well suited to editorial body text, long-form publishing and book design.
foundry_blurb: Founders Grotesk is described by Klim as a contemporary amalgamation of classic grotesks, drawing on Millar & Richard's early 20th-century Grotesque series for rudimentary geometry, serpentine curves and narrow apertures, with further details from H. W. Caslon's Doric series. These are paired with tight spacing strategies taken from Helvetica's Halbfett headline metal cuts of the late 1950s. The family is not intended as a strict revival but resolves the best details from the last century into a large family designed for modern typography.

name: Heldane Display
classification_notes: Heldane draws on 16th-century French and Flemish punchcutters including van den Keere, Garamont and Granjon, placing it firmly in the renaissance serif tradition, refined with contemporary digital drawing for display use.
summary: A display serif synthesising details from van den Keere, Garamont, Granjon and de Colines into a single rigorous family.
description: Rather than reviving a single historical model, Heldane Display distils the best letterform details from four 16th-century French and Flemish type designers into one cohesive family. Six styles run across three weights from Regular to Bold, each with a companion italic featuring swash capital alternates. The result sits comfortably in editorial, cultural institution and identity work where a grounded classical voice is needed without the stiffness of a straight revival.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Heldane as a contemporary serif family inspired by the renaissance works of Hendrik van den Keere, Claude Garamont, Robert Granjon and Simon de Colines. Rather than emulating a specific font, it amalgamates the best details from these sources into a cohesive whole, with classical typographic foundations refined through rigorous digital drawing.

name: Heldane Text
classification_notes: Heldane Text sits squarely in the garalde tradition, drawing on renaissance sources including Van den Keere, Garamont, Granjon and de Colines, with the Text cut specifically optimised for small sizes and closer proportions to Sabon.
summary: A text-optimised cut of Heldane drawn for small sizes, with denser proportions and longer serifs than its Display counterpart.
description: Heldane Text pulls from the same garalde sources as the Display cuts — Van den Keere, Garamont, Granjon and de Colines — but tempers the sharper details that break down at small sizes. Letterform widths are more even, serifs are longer, and the overall proportions move closer to Sabon, though Sowersby deliberately avoided Sabon's lighter elegance in favour of the denser, darker texture of Stempel's Garamond. Available in three weights with matching italics, the family is built for sustained reading rather than display use.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Heldane Text as optimised for small sizes, retaining the dark and sharp atmosphere of the Display cuts while moderating details that fall apart at smaller settings. Sowersby notes the proportions are closer to Sabon, but the intent was a denser, more substantial feel — akin to Stempel's Garamond by Rudolf Wolf — avoiding the light elegance of other Garamond revivals.

name: Karbon
classification_notes: Klim describe Karbon as an open, geometric sans serif with a spartan finish, explicitly referencing Futura's reductionism channelled through Gill Sans proportions and a nod to Tschichold's Uhertype sans-serif. The Karbon Slab Stencil extension into slab territory is a sub-family, not the primary classification.
summary: A geometric sans with a spartan, reductionist character, drawing on Futura's proportional logic filtered through Gill Sans and a trace of Tschichold's Uhertype.
description: Karbon sits in the tradition of early twentieth-century geometric sans-serifs, reworking Futura's stripped-back forms through the wider, more humanist proportions associated with Gill Sans. The family runs across seven weights from Hairline to Bold, each with a matching italic, giving 14 styles in total. A companion sub-family, Karbon Slab Stencil, carries the same spare linework into slab-serif territory with stencil breaks that reference industrial letterform conventions.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Karbon as an open, geometric sans serif with a contemporary spartan finish, framing it as an exploration of Paul Renner's reductionist Futura concept channelled through Eric Gill's proportions, with a slight nod to Jan Tschichold's Uhertype sans-serif. The Karbon Slab Stencil extension takes the spartan linework into slab-serif territory, with stencil breaks referencing the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms.

name: Maelstrom
classification_notes: Maelstrom belongs to the reversed-stress genre, where horizontal strokes are heavy and vertical strokes are thin, inverting conventional stroke contrast. This places it firmly in display territory, used for poster and headline work rather than text setting.
summary: A reversed-stress display typeface that inverts conventional stroke contrast to produce a striking, perverse weight distribution suited to headline and poster work.
description: Maelstrom flips the standard relationship between thick and thin strokes, producing razor-thin verticals set against heavy horizontals that carve narrow strips of light through the black mass. Its companion, Maelstrom Sans, amplifies the unease by combining heavy horizontals with spindly diagonals, giving the impression that the letters could splinter under their own tension. Both belong to a genre that sits permanently outside mainstream fashion yet refuses to disappear.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Maelstrom and Maelstrom Sans as reversed-stress typefaces that are deliberately perverse, belonging to a genre destined to be a perpetual typographic outsider — never fashionable yet never abandoned. Maelstrom's razor-thin spacing creates narrow strips of light through a heavy black mass, while Maelstrom Sans's mix of heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals gives it a fragile, about-to-splinter quality.

name: Karbon Slab Stencil
classification_notes: Classified as a slab serif due to its stencil-cut slab letterforms, extending the geometric sans Karbon into slab-serif territory with programmatic stencil breaks referencing industrial heritage.
summary: A stencil slab serif extending the geometric logic of Karbon, with break patterns drawn from the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms.
description: Karbon Slab Stencil takes the open, geometric spartan character of Karbon and pushes it into slab-serif territory, applying a logical programme of stencil cuts that reference industrial lettering traditions. Two weights, Regular and Bold, are offered with no italic counterparts, keeping the family tight and purposeful. The stencil breaks feel considered rather than ornamental, holding the geometric rigour of the parent family throughout.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Karbon as an open, geometric sans serif with a spartan finish, exploring Paul Renner's reductionist Futura concept through the proportions of Eric Gill's eponymous sans, with a nod to Jan Tschichold's Uhertype. Karbon Slab Stencil extends that spartan linework into slab-serif territory, with a logical programme of breaks referencing the industrial heritage of stencilled letterforms.

name: Maelstrom Sans
classification_notes: Maelstrom Sans is a reversed-stress display typeface, a genre defined by thick horizontals and thin verticals that invert conventional stroke contrast logic. The heavy horizontals combined with spindly diagonals place it firmly in display territory.
summary: A single-weight reversed-stress display face whose heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals create a sense of fragility bordering on structural collapse.
description: Reversed-stress typefaces invert the conventional logic of stroke contrast, and Maelstrom Sans pushes this to an unsettling extreme: thick horizontals dominate while diagonals remain threadlike, giving the type a tense, almost precarious quality. Available in one weight, Bold, it belongs to a typographic tradition that Klim describe as a perpetual outsider genre, never fashionable yet never fully abandoned. Its uses span branding, posters and campaign work where conventional type would read as too safe or too polished.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Maelstrom Sans as a reversed-stress typeface, part of a genre they call perverse but charming. Its uneasy combination of heavy horizontals and spindly diagonals makes it feel fragile, as though it could splinter at any moment, and they position it as a typographic perpetual outsider, never fashionable yet never abandoned.

name: Mānuka Slab
classification_notes: A compressed slab serif drawing on 19th century German wood type with tight spacing and sharp joins, placing it squarely in the slab/display tradition with a compressed width emphasis.
summary: A compressed slab serif for large sizes, grafting 19th century German wood type details onto a contemporary antipodean sensibility.
description: Seven weights from Thin to Ultra, all compressed and intended for headline and display use. Deviant details drawn from Teutonic timber type give the family a distinctive texture: tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins that create a dense, sparkling rhythm at large sizes. Part of a wider Mānuka collection that includes standard and condensed cuts.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Mānuka Slab as new growth from old wood, a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes. With deviant details pilfered from Teutonic timber type, it grafts a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th century German root-stock. Tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins make a compelling texture, like sunlight sparkling through a forest canopy.

name: Mānuka
classification_notes: A compressed display typeface drawing on 19th-century German timber type, with tight spacing and closed apertures that place it firmly in the display category. Its compressed proportions and deviant wood-type details resist a simple grotesque classification.
summary: A compressed display family grafting antipodean sensibility onto 19th-century German timber type, across seven weights from Thin to Ultra.
description: Seven weights from Thin to Ultra, all built for large sizes with tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins borrowed from Teutonic wood type. The result is a dense, sparkling texture that carries a distinctly New Zealand character despite its Germanic roots. Seen in use across branding, editorial and cultural identities, often alongside companion families Mānuka Condensed and Mānuka Slab.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Mānuka as new growth from old wood: a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes that pilfers deviant details from Teutonic timber type, grafting a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th-century German root-stock. Tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins create a compelling texture, likened to sunlight sparkling through a forest canopy.

name: Mānuka Condensed
classification_notes: A compressed display family drawing on 19th-century German wood type, with closed apertures, sharp joins and tight spacing that place it firmly in display rather than text use. The deviant details and condensed structure push it beyond standard grotesque classification.
summary: A compressed display family grafting 19th-century German wood type details onto a contemporary antipodean sensibility, built for large sizes.
description: Seven weights from Thin to Ultra, each designed with tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins that create a dense, distinctive texture at poster and headline scales. The design draws on Teutonic timber type as its historical root-stock, reinterpreting those deviant letterforms through a contemporary New Zealand lens. Seen in use across exhibition graphics, cultural institutions and editorial work where compressed display type needs both historical weight and a modern edge.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Mānuka Condensed as new growth from old wood — a collection of compressed fonts for large sizes that grafts a contemporary antipodean aesthetic onto 19th-century German root-stock. Deviant details pilfered from Teutonic timber type combine with tight spacing, closed apertures and sharp joins to create a compelling texture, like sunlight sparkling through a forest canopy.

name: Metric
classification_notes: Metric blends geometric construction with humanist optical corrections, placing it between pure geometric sans and humanist sans traditions, inspired directly by the monoline lettering of West Berlin street signs.
summary: A geometric humanist sans drawn from West Berlin street sign lettering, where monoline construction is tempered by careful optical correction.
description: Designed alongside Calibre, sharing the same underlying geometry but diverging in the treatment of key letterforms. The West Berlin signage source gives it an unpretentious, rational quality that stops short of the cold precision typical of engineered grotesques. Fourteen styles span Thin to Black with matching italics, making it a capable workhorse for both text and display settings.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Metric as a geometric, humanist sans serif inspired by lettering on West Berlin street signs, noting that unlike most engineered signage lettering these forms are elegant and clear. The monoline construction feels rational and unpretentious, and typographic optical corrections gently temper geometric tendencies, producing letterforms that are contemporary and crisp. Metric was designed alongside Calibre, sharing a fundamental geometry but differing in the finish of key letterforms.

name: National
classification_notes: National draws on the Akzidenz Grotesk era of European grotesque sans serifs, with old-style figures, a true italic and looser spacing that distinguish it from the more mechanical neo-grotesque tradition represented by Helvetica.
summary: A grotesque sans drawn in opposition to Helvetica, taking its cues from Akzidenz Grotesk with looser spacing, unambiguous letterforms and old-style figures for long-form reading.
description: Conceived as a stylistic counterpoint to Helvetica, National favours the humble, pre-neutral character of Akzidenz Grotesk over mid-century Swiss rigidity. Its true italic, old-style figures and deliberately loose spacing make it well-suited to extended text at small sizes rather than display work. Eighteen styles span nine weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe National as a deceptively simple sans serif whose subtle details give it a distinctive but unobtrusive personality. It was drawn as a stylistic opposite to Helvetica, with looser spacing, unambiguous forms, old-style figures and a true italic, all aimed at making it suitable for long-form reading at small sizes. Its details reference the Akzidenz Grotesk era, lending it a humble, workmanlike character designed to function in contemporary typographic conditions.

name: Martina Plantijn
classification_notes: Directly derived from Plantin, itself a revival of 16th-century types documented at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp; the family sits firmly in the old-style serif tradition with humanist roman and italic cuts updated for digital use.
summary: A digital reimagining of Plantin, grounded in Frank Hinman Pierpont's original research into 16th-century Antwerp types and refined across five weights with matching italics.
description: Built from Pierpont's workhorse Plantin and the archival material at the Plantin-Moretus Museum, Martina Plantijn makes considered corrections to the roman and italic that digital typography had long left unaddressed. Ten styles run from Light to Black with matching italics throughout. The typeface takes its name from Martina Plantijn, daughter of printer Christophe Plantin and wife of Jan Moretus, who ran the Officina Plantiniana after her husband's death in 1610.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Martina Plantijn as a better Plantin, informed by the workhorse qualities of Frank Hinman Pierpont's typeface and expanding upon his research of 16th-century type at the Plantin-Moretus Museum in Antwerp, making decisive digital updates across its roman and italic cuts.

name: National 2 Condensed
classification_notes: National 2 Condensed is a width variant of National 2, a grotesque sans whose details draw from the Akzidenz-Grotesk era, giving it a workmanlike, utilitarian character consistent with early 20th-century European grotesque tradition.
summary: The condensed width variant of National 2, a thoroughly redrawn grotesque with Akzidenz-era roots and a measured, workaday tone.
description: National 2 Condensed is one of three new widths added in the complete overhaul of Klim's National, with every letterform redrawn and remastered over a decade. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, all carrying the humble, workmanlike character drawn from the Akzidenz-Grotesk period. In use it appears across editorial, identity and digital work where a reliable grotesque with tight horizontal economy is needed.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of National, 10 years in the making, with every letter redrawn and remastered. The weight distribution is improved, curve quality refined, and three new widths — Narrow, Condensed and Compressed — have been added. National's details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character with an agreeable tone of voice.

name: National 2
classification_notes: National 2 draws explicitly from the Akzidenz-Grotesk era, giving it the workmanlike character of early 20th-century European grotesques. Klim describe it as a humble sans with an agreeable tone of voice, firmly placing it in the grotesque tradition.
summary: A thorough redrawing of Klim's National, with improved weight distribution, refined curves and three new width variants added across 16 styles.
description: Every letter in National 2 has been redrawn from scratch, refining the curve quality and weight distribution of the original National over a decade of development. The family covers eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and expands into Narrow, Condensed and Compressed widths for demanding editorial and information design contexts. Its details are rooted in the Akzidenz-Grotesk era, lending it a quiet, workmanlike character suited to long-form text and identity work alike.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of their popular sans National, ten years in the making, with every single letter redrawn and remastered. Weight distribution is improved, curve quality is refined, and three new widths — Narrow, Condensed and Compressed — have been added. National's details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character with an agreeable tone of voice.

name: National 2 Narrow
classification_notes: National 2 Narrow draws from the Akzidenz-Grotesk tradition, placing it firmly in the grotesque sans-serif category; the narrow width is a distinct cut of the National 2 family rather than a separate typeface.
summary: A narrow-width cut of National 2, Kris Sowersby's complete redraw of National, offering 16 styles rooted in the Akzidenz-Grotesk tradition with refined curves and improved weight distribution.
description: National 2 Narrow is one of three new widths added to National 2, the decade-long overhaul of Klim's original National. Every letterform has been redrawn from scratch, with tighter curve quality and better weight distribution than the original. Sixteen styles run from Thin to Black with matching italics, giving the narrow width the same full range as the standard cut. Its character draws from the Akzidenz-Grotesk era: humble, workmanlike, with an even tone suited to editorial and typographic systems that demand space efficiency.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe National 2 as a complete overhaul of National, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and remastered. The weight distribution is improved, curve quality refined, and three new widths added including Narrow. Its details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character with an agreeable tone of voice.

name: Pitch
classification_notes: Pitch is a monospaced typeface explicitly designed to evoke typewriter aesthetics, making mono the clear category; its synthesis of analogue typewriter texture with digital refinement places it in a typewriter-inspired subcategory.
summary: A monospaced typeface that synthesises digital precision with analogue typewriter character across five weights and matching italics.
description: Rather than emulating typewritten text literally, Pitch distils the aesthetic atmosphere of the typewriter into a clean, controlled monospaced family. Ten styles span Light through Bold with corresponding italics, giving the family range for both body text and display use. It sits comfortably in editorial, literary and interface contexts where fixed-width rhythm and tactile warmth are both required.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Pitch as a love letter to the typewriter, a machine with a singular purpose of putting letters on a page. They frame it as a synthesis of digital and analogue that evokes rather than emulates typewritten text, capturing the tactile, immediate quality of stabbing at keys and watching letters appear.

name: Newzald
classification_notes: Newzald draws from classic Dutch typefaces by J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman, placing it firmly in the Dutch transitional tradition with its large x-height and slightly condensed proportions optimised for text setting.
summary: A Dutch-influenced text serif with a large x-height and slightly condensed proportions, designed for demanding editorial environments.
description: Drawing on the Dutch typeface tradition of J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman, Newzald is built for economy and legibility in column-heavy editorial settings. Its four weights run from Book to Black, each with a matching italic, giving typographers a contained but versatile palette. The slightly condensed forms allow dense setting without appearing cramped.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Newzald as a hardworking text typeface for the international editorial environment, its economical rigour derived from classic Dutch typefaces by J.F. Rosart and J.M. Fleischman. A large x-height and slightly condensed forms allow more words per column without sacrificing legibility, and the four-weight range from Book to Black offers a dependable palette for the discerning typographer.

name: National 2 Compressed
classification_notes: National 2 Compressed draws from the tradition of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a workmanlike grotesque character; the Compressed cut is an extreme width variant of the overhauled National 2 family.
summary: The compressed width variant of National 2, a fully redrawn grotesque sans rooted in the Akzidenz-Grotesk tradition, offered in 16 styles across eight weights.
description: National 2 Compressed is the tightest of four width variants in the National 2 family, a complete overhaul of Klim's original National sans that took a decade to complete. Every letter was redrawn, with improved weight distribution and refined curve quality, and the family now spans Narrow, Condensed and Compressed widths alongside the standard cut. The Akzidenz-Grotesk influence gives the typeface a humble, workmanlike tone suited to editorial, signage and high-impact display work.
foundry_blurb: National 2 is described by Klim as a complete overhaul of their popular National sans, ten years in the making, with every letter redrawn and remastered. The weight distribution is improved, curve quality refined, and three new widths added: Narrow, Condensed and Compressed. Its details are drawn from the era of Akzidenz-Grotesk, giving it a humble, workmanlike character with an agreeable tone of voice.

name: Söhne
classification_notes: Söhne is explicitly positioned as a neo-grotesque, framed as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk filtered through Helvetica, drawing directly on the analogue materiality of the NYC Subway's Unimark wayfinding system.
summary: A neo-grotesque that synthesises the warmth of Akzidenz-Grotesk with the discipline of Helvetica, rooted in the analogue materiality of Unimark's NYC Subway signage.
description: Sixteen styles span eight weights from Extraleicht to Extrafett, each paired with a kursiv, all named in German after the tradition of the source material. The design holds the optical texture of metal-era grotesques while meeting the systematic rigour associated with mid-century Swiss typography. Its reference point is the specific character of "Standard Medium" as it appeared in Unimark's subway system, not an abstracted or cleaned-up version of it.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of Standard Medium as used in Unimark's legendary wayfinding system for the NYC Subway.

name: Pitch Sans
classification_notes: Pitch Sans is derived from the Pitch typewriter-inspired family, retaining the fixed-width aesthetic of typewriter letterforms but presented as a proportional or monospaced sans without serifs. Its origins in typewriter typology place it firmly in the monospaced sans category.
summary: A sans-serif counterpart to the typewriter-inspired Pitch family, offering fixed-width aesthetics across five weights with matching italics.
description: Derived from Klim's Pitch family, which takes the tactile aesthetic of typewriter lettering as its starting point, Pitch Sans strips back the serif elements whilst retaining the monospaced rhythm and analogue character of the original. Ten styles span five weights from Light to Bold, each with an italic, making it unusually versatile for a typeface rooted in typewriter typology. It suits editorial settings, coding environments and any context where a mechanical or archival quality is wanted without the overt historicism of a slab.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Pitch as a love letter to the typewriter — tactile, analogue and immediate. The family synthesises digital and analogue qualities to evoke, rather than directly emulate, typewritten text, capturing the singular aesthetic atmosphere of a machine designed purely to put letters on a page.

name: Signifier
classification_notes: Signifier is rooted in 17th-century Fell Types, placing it within the transitional serif tradition, though Sowersby's Brutalist approach and digital vector logic give it a distinctly contemporary character.
summary: A Brutalist recasting of the 17th-century Fell Types by Kris Sowersby, rebuilt from Bézier curves and sharp vectors shaped by machine logic.
description: Sowersby worked consciously with the computer to translate the material properties of lead, antimony and tin from the Fell Types into digital form, letting machine logic and a Brutalist ethos determine the resulting curves and vectors. The family spans 14 styles across seven weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic, and is available as a variable font. The result sits in the territory between historical scholarship and contemporary editorial design.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Signifier as a Brutalist response to 17th-century typefaces, with its digital immateriality drawn from a deeply material past. Sowersby worked consciously with the computer to recast the lead, antimony and tin of the Fell Types into ones and zeros, with Bézier curves and sharp vectors determined by machine logic and a Brutalist ethos.

name: Söhne Schmal
classification_notes: A condensed variant of Söhne, itself a grotesque rooted in Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica's influence on 20th-century wayfinding and graphic systems. The narrow width maintains the same neo-grotesque DNA.
summary: The condensed cut of Söhne, a grotesque shaped by Akzidenz-Grotesk and the analogue materiality of Unimark's NYC Subway signage system.
description: Söhne Schmal brings the narrow width to Klim's Söhne family, which is framed as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk filtered through the reality of Helvetica. Sixteen styles run from Extraleicht to Extrafett across upright and italic cuts, giving considerable range for tight measures and poster work. The alternate single-storey 'a', drawn from the original metal cuts of Akzidenz-Grotesk, is available across all styles via OpenType.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of 'Standard Medium' as used in Unimark's legendary wayfinding system for the NYC Subway. Söhne Schmal is the condensed expression of that concept, offered in 16 styles.

name: Söhne Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of Söhne, itself a neo-grotesque rooted in Akzidenz-Grotesk and Helvetica; the mono classification follows directly from its fixed-width spacing and grotesque skeleton.
summary: A monospaced variant of Söhne, drawing on the analogue materiality of Akzidenz-Grotesk as used in Unimark's NYC Subway wayfinding system.
description: Söhne Mono carries the fixed-width discipline of a coding typeface while retaining the neo-grotesque warmth of its parent family, which Klim describe as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica. Sixteen styles span eight weights from Extraleicht to Extrafett, each paired with a Kursiv, giving the family an unusually broad tonal range for a monospaced design. The German weight names and the Unimark subway reference anchor it firmly in mid-century European typographic history.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Söhne Mono as capturing the analogue materiality of 'Standard Medium' as used in Unimark's legendary wayfinding system for the NYC Subway, with the typeface conceived as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica.

name: Söhne Breit
classification_notes: Söhne Breit is a wide variant of Söhne, itself a neo-grotesque rooted in Akzidenz-Grotesk and filtered through the aesthetic of Helvetica as used in the Unimark NYC Subway wayfinding system.
summary: The wide variant of Söhne, drawing on Akzidenz-Grotesk's analogue materiality as filtered through Helvetica and the Unimark NYC Subway system.
description: Söhne Breit extends the Söhne family into an expanded width, offering sixteen styles across eight weights from Extraleicht to Extrafett, each with a matching italic. The family carries German weight names and takes its formal cues from the 'Standard Medium' used in Unimark's iconic New York subway wayfinding — capturing the tactile, analogue quality of that era's signage rather than digital refinement. Its wide proportions make it well suited to headlines, editorial display and identities that want the authority of a mid-century neo-grotesque at generous measure.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Söhne as the memory of Akzidenz-Grotesk framed through the reality of Helvetica, capturing the analogue materiality of 'Standard Medium' used in Unimark's legendary wayfinding system for the NYC Subway. Söhne Breit is the wide-width variant of that family.

name: Tiempos Fine
classification_notes: Part of the Tiempos Collection, Tiempos Fine is a modern serif with proportions rooted in Plantin and Times, but refined for large display sizes with heightened contrast between warm curves and sharp details, placing it squarely in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: The display-optimised cut of the Tiempos family, sharpening the contrast between warm curves and fine details for large headline sizes.
description: Tiempos Fine shares the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline but pushes the interplay between its warm curves and sharp details further, making it suited to larger, more refined editorial settings. The family runs across six weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, and is available as a variable font. It sits at the top of the Tiempos Collection, which also includes Tiempos Text for body copy and Tiempos Headline for intermediary display use.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Tiempos Fine as keeping the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline while intensifying the interplay between warm curves and sharp details. It is the most refined member of the Tiempos Collection, a modern serif family built for editorial typography and rooted in the functionality of Plantin and Times, updated for contemporary use.

name: The Future
classification_notes: A geometric sans-serif derived directly from Futura's production drawings, retaining the circular, constructivist geometry and avant-garde alternates that define Renner's original design.
summary: A precise homage to Paul Renner's Futura, drawn from original production drawings with a companion monospaced variant reimagined as a speculative typewriter face.
description: Working from Futura's original production drawings, Klim have reconstructed Renner's geometric architecture and preserved the avant-garde alternate characters that distinguish the source material from its many imitators. The family runs 14 styles across seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics. The Future Mono extends the concept speculatively, reimagining the geometric sans as a typewriter face attributed to Kyota Sugimoto, inventor of the Japanese typewriter.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe The Future as a homage to Futura, Paul Renner's progressive classic, worked up from original production drawings to carefully observe its geometric architecture and avant-garde alternates. They frame it as an articulation of Renner's thinking on modernity, abstraction and purity, with The Future Mono taking a speculative turn by reimagining Futura as a typewriter font commissioned by Kyota Sugimoto.

name: The Future Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced typeface modelled on Futura's geometric architecture, reimagined as a typewriter font; the fixed-width constraint and typewriter conceit place it firmly in the mono category with geometric sans origins.
summary: A speculative reimagining of Futura as a typewriter font, framed as a commission by Japanese typewriter inventor Kyota Sugimoto.
description: Working from original Futura production drawings, The Future Mono applies Futura's geometric rigour and avant-garde alternates to a fixed-width format, treating the typewriter as its fictional origin. The conceit imagines Kyota Sugimoto, who patented the first practical Japanese typewriter in 1915, commissioning a Futura-derived machine face. Fourteen styles run across seven weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, carrying the full range of Renner's modernist vocabulary into a monospaced structure.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe The Future Mono as a speculative reimagining of Futura as a typewriter font commissioned by Kyota Sugimoto, part of The Future family which pays homage to Paul Renner's progressive classic by working from original production drawings to observe Futura's geometric architecture and avant-garde alternates.

name: Tiempos Headline
classification_notes: Tiempos Headline is a display-oriented serif descended from the Plantin and Times tradition, refined for large headline sizes with a balance of practicality and elegance, placing it firmly in the transitional serif category.
summary: A display-weight serif drawn for headline use, balancing sharp editorial crispness with warm curves inherited from the Plantin and Times tradition.
description: Part of the broader Tiempos Collection, this cut is optimised for larger sizes where its interplay between sharp details and warm curves reads most clearly. Six weights from Light to Black, each with italics, give designers a full range for editorial hierarchies. Snug spacing and considered proportions make it well suited to magazine, brand and newspaper headline work.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Tiempos Headline as designed for larger headline sizes, striking a balance between practicality and elegance. It sits alongside Tiempos Text, which updates the functionality of Plantin and Times for contemporary use, and Tiempos Fine, which keeps the proportions and letterfit of Tiempos Headline while intensifying the interplay between warm curves and sharp details.

name: Tiempos Text
classification_notes: Tiempos Text takes its functional cues from Plantin and Times New Roman, two canonical transitional serifs, updating their legibility-focused proportions and ink-spread compensation for contemporary editorial use.
summary: A text-optimised serif drawn from the tradition of Plantin and Times, adjusted for contemporary editorial typesetting across eight styles including a variable font axis.
description: Tiempos Text sits within the broader Tiempos Collection, focusing squarely on body copy performance at small sizes. Its proportions are narrowed and spacing tightened relative to headline cuts, with contrast calibrated to account for ink-spread. Four weights from Regular to Bold each carry a matching italic, and the family ships as a variable font with a continuous weight axis.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Tiempos Text as taking the functionality of Plantin and Times and gently updating it for contemporary use, calling it robust and clear, and ideal for economic and legible typesetting. It forms part of the wider Tiempos Collection alongside Tiempos Headline and Tiempos Fine.

name: Untitled Sans
classification_notes: Klim explicitly classify Untitled Sans as a neogrotesk, originating from a rationalised, stripped-back reworking of National. The design philosophy deliberately erases any individual designer's hand, placing it squarely in the neogrotesque tradition.
summary: A deliberately anonymous neogrotesk by Kris Sowersby, conceived with Duncan Forbes and validated by Jasper Morrison and Naoto Fukasawa's Super Normal philosophy.
description: Developed over several years from a rationalised offshoot of National, Untitled Sans is designed to carry no individual designer's fingerprints, asking little of the typographer while performing reliably across contexts from spreadsheets to festival identities. Ten styles span Light to Black with matching italics, and the family is offered as a variable font. Its plainness is deliberate: Sowersby frames neogrotesk as the typographic equivalent of Muzak, present and functional without demanding attention.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Untitled Sans as a quotidian typeface, a plain neogrotesk validated by the Super Normal project of Jasper Morrison and Naoto Fukasawa. It started as a rationalised version of National with working title 'National Plain', evolved through an intentionally detached, mechanical drawing process, and was designed to have a deliberate aesthetic of not being designed by anyone in particular.

name: Untitled Serif
classification_notes: Sowersby explicitly situates Untitled Serif within the old-style genre, drawing on post-Caslon, pre-Times jobbing types with Monotype Imprint as a primary model, featuring gentle stress and restrained detailing rather than Venetian or Modern extremes.
summary: A deliberately anonymous old-style serif drawn from the post-Caslon, pre-Times tradition of plain jobbing types, designed to be utterly unnoticeable in text.
description: Kris Sowersby modelled Untitled Serif on Monotype's Imprint of 1912, a regularised Caslon derivative with a larger x-height and gentle stress that sits between Renaissance and Modern extremes. The family runs across three weights with matching italics, and every design decision was tested by reading updated fonts embedded in an ePub of Orwell's 1984 and removing any detail that drew attention to itself. The result is a text serif that carries no fashionable newness, no ironic age and no overt historical connotations.
foundry_blurb: Klim describe Untitled Serif as a quotidian typeface drawn from the old-style genre, specifically the post-Caslon, pre-Times workhorses that were offered by nearly every metal type foundry of the era. Sowersby used Monotype's Imprint as a primary model and conceived the typeface as an intellectual challenge: a serif that sets text well without feeling fashionably new or ironically old, and which carries no layers of history or cultural signifiers beyond its own function. He tested each round of changes by reading chapters of 1984 in the updated font, removing any detail that stood out, until the typeface felt entirely normal to read.
